text: The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon away from the pelvis, ensuring it remains outside the surgical field to facilitate active participation by the assistant during the pelvic surgery.

The surgeon opens the space between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament to access the uterine artery at its origin. The uterine artery is identified and isolated from the ureter below, with the fascia dissected away. The uterine artery is then coagulated, and the same procedure is performed on the opposite side, reducing vascularity and minimizing blood loss during the myomectomy.

The surgeon makes an incision in the uterus and inserts a myoma screw into the fibroid to provide traction and counter traction, facilitating the enucleation of the deep intramural fibroid from its bed. The fibroid is completely separated, revealing the intact endometrium cavity.

The surgeon closes the myoma bed using multilayered barbed sutures. Continuous sutures are applied to the deeper layer, followed by ipsilateral or contralateral baseball suturing for the superficial layer. The barbed sutures are visible, reducing exposure to the peritoneal cavity and minimizing the risk of adhesions. The surgical field remains bloodless.
The surgeon completes suturing the uterine incision with minimal blood loss, ensuring the entire opening is closed.
